meta.name: Classic Brioche Loaf
meta.subtitle: Rich, tender, buttery crumb • great for French toast & sandwiches • fresh milled or commercial flour

source.notes: Standard enriched brioche formulated for a single loaf pan. Includes fresh-milled guidance, overnight option, and stand-mixer or hand-knead paths.

yield.notes: Makes 1 loaf (8.5x4.5 in / 9x5 in pan). Typically ~12 slices.

tasteProfile.notes: Buttery aroma, soft crumb, slightly sweet—excellent for toast and French toast.

ingredients.label: Flour (bread flour preferred; or all-purpose; or fresh milled wheat flour—see Fresh-Milled Notes)
ingredients.notes: Bread flour strengthens structure for high butter/egg content. All-purpose works but is slightly softer/weaker.

ingredients.label: Instant yeast (or active dry yeast)
ingredients.notes: About 2 1/4 teaspoons. If using active dry, bloom in the warm milk first (see steps).

ingredients.label: Milk, warm (95–105°F / 35–40°C)
ingredients.notes: About 1/2 cup. Do not overheat or it can harm yeast.

ingredients.label: Large eggs
ingredients.notes: About 150g total egg. Use room temperature if possible.

ingredients.label: Unsalted butter, softened (very soft but not melted)
ingredients.notes: About 12 tablespoons (1 1/2 sticks). Add gradually for best dough development.

ingredients.label: Egg (for egg wash)
ingredients.notes: Whisk with a splash of milk or water. Optional but recommended for shine.

steps.title: Optional: bloom active dry yeast (if using)
instructions: If using active dry yeast, stir yeast into the warm milk with a pinch of the sugar (use from the recipe sugar).
Rest 5–10 minutes until foamy. If using instant yeast, skip blooming.

steps.title: Mix and develop the dough (before butter)
instructions: In a mixer bowl (or large bowl), combine flour, sugar, salt, and yeast (instant).
Add warm milk and eggs. Mix until a rough dough forms, then knead until the dough begins to look smoother and stretchy.
Stand mixer: 4–6 minutes on low to medium-low with dough hook.
By hand: 8–10 minutes kneading until dough is cohesive (it will still be firm before butter).

steps.title: Add butter gradually (key brioche step)
instructions: With mixer running on low (or kneading by hand), add softened butter 1–2 tablespoons at a time.
Let each addition incorporate before adding more. The dough may look greasy/sloppy at first—keep mixing.
Continue until dough becomes smooth, glossy, elastic, and pulls away from the bowl (though it may still be tacky).
Stand mixer: ~10–14 minutes total butter incorporation time.
Target dough temp after mixing: ~76–82°F (24–28°C) if possible.

steps.title: First rise (bulk) or chill overnight
instructions: Place dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and rise until puffy and roughly doubled, 60–90 minutes (warmer rooms rise faster).
For best flavor and easier shaping: after 30–60 minutes at room temp, refrigerate dough 8–16 hours (overnight).

steps.title: Shape loaf
instructions: Butter or line a loaf pan.
Turn dough out onto a lightly floured surface. If very soft, chill 20 minutes first.
For a classic brioche loaf: divide into 4 equal pieces, round into tight balls, and place side-by-side in the pan.
Alternative: shape into a single log and place seam-side down.

steps.title: Final proof
instructions: Cover and proof until dough rises close to the rim of the pan and looks very puffy, 60–120 minutes depending on temperature.
If the dough was cold from the fridge, proof will take longer.

steps.title: Egg wash (optional) and bake
instructions: Heat oven to 350°F (175°C).
Optional: whisk egg wash (1 egg + splash of milk/water) and brush gently over the loaf.
Bake 30–40 minutes until deep golden brown.
Internal temp target: 190–200°F (88–93°C). If browning too fast, tent loosely with foil for the last 10 minutes.

steps.title: Cool before slicing
instructions: Cool in pan 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack and cool at least 45 minutes before slicing.
Brioche slices best once fully cooled (crumb sets as it cools).

freshMilledNotes.summary: Brioche is easier with strong flour. Fresh milled flour can work, but benefits from blending and/or sifting to improve softness and extensibility.
guidelines: Best results: use 70% fresh milled flour + 30% bread flour (same total flour weight).
If using 100% fresh milled (unsifted), expect a slightly tighter crumb and longer mixing time; consider sifting out some bran for tenderness.
Fresh milled flour can absorb more liquid—add 10–25g extra milk only if dough is too firm after butter is fully incorporated.
Longer cold fermentation (overnight) improves handling and crumb for fresh milled brioche.

variations.name: Brioche buns
variations.change: Divide into 8–10 pieces, shape into buns, proof until puffy, bake at 350°F (175°C) for 18–22 minutes.

variations.name: Orange brioche
variations.change: Add zest of 1 orange and 5g orange extract (optional).
variations.notes: Excellent for breakfast and holiday use.

variations.name: Cinnamon sugar brioche loaf
variations.change: Roll dough into a rectangle, sprinkle with cinnamon sugar, roll up, and pan-proof seam-side down.
variations.notes: More like a breakfast sweet loaf.

storage.counter: Cool completely; store airtight up to 3 days. Brioche dries faster than lean breads—keep well sealed.
storage.fridge: Not ideal (stales faster). If needed, wrap very tightly and rewarm slices.
storage.freezer: Freeze whole loaf or slices up to 2 months. Thaw at room temp; toast slices to refresh.

notes: Brioche dough can look like it’s breaking when butter is added—keep mixing and it will come together.
If dough is very sticky: chill 15–30 minutes before shaping; avoid adding lots of extra flour (can make loaf dry).
For best French toast: use brioche that’s 1–2 days old or lightly dried in a low oven.

audit.testNotes: Schema-compliant: schemaVersion+meta present; ingredients include id+label+amount; steps include id+title+instructions; extra fields allowed via meta/source & other permissive objects.
